does: This paper proposes GenRM, an iterative algorithm that trains language models to generate reasoning traces for self-generated preference labels that better align with human judgments. The core contribution addresses a key limitation of RLAIF: synthetic preference labels from LLMs often diverge significantly from human preferences. GenRM works by having an LLM iteratively reason through preference comparisons, generating explanations that improve label quality. The paper demonstrates empirically that naive zero-shot LLM-based preference judgments underperform Bradley-Terry reward models by 9-36% on in-distribution tasks, while GenRM closes this gap and achieves comparable in-distribution accuracy to supervised reward models while substantially outperforming them on out-of-distribution tasks.
connects: This work builds directly on the RLHF pipeline established for modern LLM alignment, which relies on training reward models from human preference judgments to guide policy optimization. It extends recent RLAIF methods that attempted to reduce human annotation costs by synthesizing preferences from LLMs themselves, but responds to empirical findings showing such synthetic labels misalign with human judgments. The paper connects to broader research on improving label quality through reasoning and chain-of-thought approaches, as well as work on Bradley-Terry models and preference modeling. It also engages with lines of research on data efficiency in alignment and the challenge of obtaining reliable training signals for reward models without extensive human annotation.
enables: GenRM opens pathways for more scalable and cost-effective RLHF pipelines by demonstrating that high-quality synthetic preference labels are achievable through iterative self-reasoning rather than external human annotation. This could significantly reduce the resource burden of aligning large models. The approach potentially enables better out-of-distribution generalization of reward models, suggesting applications where preference judgments must transfer beyond training data. Future work could explore whether such self-reasoned preferences provide signal for other alignment objectives beyond preference modeling, how reasoning traces themselves could be optimized, and whether the method scales to larger models and more diverse preference domains. The technique might also inform research on self-improvement in LLMs and the use of internal model reasoning for generating higher-quality training targets.
